Costa Rica Histórica

Puente Río GrandeEl Puente del Río Grande

Three documentary views of a Pacific Railway crossing, from construction cableway to finished span to a National Geographic plate — c. 1900–1922

A single bridge, seen three ways: the Ferrocarril al Pacífico's crossing of the Río Grande, captured first mid-construction by aerial cableway, then complete and in service, then again as a syndicated press illustration for an American readership.

PRG-01

Puente "Rio Grande" en construcciónThe Río Grande Bridge, under construction

Paynter Bros., San José — two-panel postcard, "Puente 'Rio Grande' en construcción"

Before the deck existed, men and materials crossed the gorge by aerial cableway. The left panel shows the cantilevered erection arm reaching out from the canyon wall, the suspension cable strung across to the opposite tower. The right panel shows the cable car itself in transit — a wooden basket suspended over open air, carrying several workmen across the span, the forested gorge falling away beneath them.

This is construction-phase documentation of a kind rarely preserved: most surviving postcards show finished infrastructure, not the temporary rigging used to build it. The téléférique method was standard practice for steel-arch and truss bridges in mountainous terrain, allowing erection crews to work from both sides of a canyon before any permanent crossing was usable.

Publisher
Paynter Bros., San José, C.R.
Title (card)
“Puente ‘Rio Grande’ en construcción”
Format
Two-panel composite postcard, decorative rose-motif border
Period
c. 1900–1910 (construction era, Ferrocarril al Pacífico)
Line
Ferrocarril al Pacífico (San José–Puntarenas)
Curator's note Paynter Bros. is a first-tier imprint in this archive — the same studio responsible for the completed-bridge card (Plate II) and for the Río Sixaola river-transit card in the Crossings catalogue. The pairing of construction and completion views from a single photographic house is unusual and valuable: it documents a single engineering project across its build sequence rather than presenting an isolated finished result.
PRG-02

Ferrocarril al Pacífico: Puente sobre el Río GrandePacific Railway: Bridge over the Río Grande

Paynter Bros. — “Ferrocarril al Pacífico: Puente sobre el Río Grande”, Memorias de Costa Rica series

The same crossing, finished and in revenue service. A mixed train — locomotive, boxcar, and passenger coaches — crosses the deck at speed, workmen visible riding the cars. Below, the steel arch that replaced the construction cableway carries the load down to massive masonry piers anchored in the canyon walls.

The arch-and-truss profile, with the parabolic lower chord meeting vertical piers, is characteristic of the steel bridge engineering exported from American and British fabricators to Central American railways in this period — the same engineering vocabulary seen elsewhere in this archive's Río Virilla crossing, also on the Pacific line.

Publisher
Paynter Bros., San José, C.R.
Series
Memorias de Costa Rica
Title (card)
“Ferrocarril al Pacífico: Puente sobre el Río Grande”
Subject
Steam locomotive and mixed train crossing completed steel arch bridge
Line
Ferrocarril al Pacífico (San José–Puntarenas)
Catalogue x-ref
T-04, Transport Costa Rica Histórica v2.7
Curator's note Already logged as T-04 in the Transport Costa Rica Histórica catalogue and cross-referenced in the Pacific Railway stations supplement. Reproduced here as the centre panel of a three-part documentary sequence on this single structure — its companion construction card (Plate I) and the press-syndicated book plate (Plate III) are not part of the Transport catalogue proper.
PRG-03

The Railway to Costa Rica's Atlantic Coast Traverses Picturesque Country“Costa Rica, Land of the Banana,” The National Geographic Magazine, p. 205

Central News Photo Service — plate accompanying Paul B. Popenoe's “Costa Rica, Land of the Banana,” The National Geographic Magazine, Vol. XLI, No. 2, February 1922, p. 205

This is the same bridge as Plates I and II — the Río Grande crossing of the Pacific Railway — seen from the canyon floor rather than from the deck or the construction cableway. Two unnamed men stand among the rocks and deadfall, pointing up at the span: staffage included to give the arch a human scale, not documentary subjects in their own right.

The bridge earned that kind of attention honestly. At 340 feet (104 m), it was the highest railway bridge ever built in Central America, and remains the highest bridge in Costa Rica. It was one of the first balanced cantilever truss bridges built anywhere — each half a self-supporting structure meeting at the crown, rather than a true arch — a design closely paralleled by France's Viaur Viaduct, completed the same year.

What's worth noting precisely here: this plate ran in the same National Geographic Magazine feature (Paul B. Popenoe's text, February 1922) that elsewhere in this archive supplied H. N. Rudd's and H. Wimmer's own field photography of Talamanca life and the Atlantic coast. This particular image, though, carries a Central News Photo Service credit rather than Rudd's or Wimmer's. Photo credits in this era track negative ownership, not magazine staffing — a credit line simply names whoever held the rights to the print, regardless of who else the publication had in the field at the time. There's no need to read a deliberate substitution into it; Central News Photo Service likely had a photographer or stringer at or near the bridge independently of Popenoe's assignment, and the magazine used the available image. Popenoe's own text, in fact, makes no mention of a bridge at all — only a brief general passage on the Atlantic-to-Pacific rail system. The caption beneath this plate is not illustrating anything Popenoe wrote; it's independent picture-desk copy, written to accompany an image the text never refers to.

Source
Paul B. Popenoe, “Costa Rica, Land of the Banana,” The National Geographic Magazine, Vol. XLI, No. 2, February 1922, p. 205
Photo credit (this plate)
Central News Photo Service — licensed, not a staff photographer credit
Caption
“The Railway to Costa Rica's Atlantic Coast Traverses Picturesque Country”
Format
Half-tone magazine plate
Period
February 1922
Bridge record
Highest railway bridge in Central America (340 ft / 104 m); highest bridge in Costa Rica
Engineering type
Balanced cantilever truss, comparable to the Viaur Viaduct, France
Curator's note Correcting an earlier working note: this is not a separate "book," and the article's author, Paul B. Popenoe, drew on Rudd's and Wimmer's first-hand photography of the Talamanca and coastal material elsewhere in this same issue. This one plate carries a different credit — not because the magazine had a gap to fill, but most likely because photo credit in this era tracked negative ownership: whoever held the rights to a given print got the line, independent of who else was in the field for the same assignment. Confirmed against the article text: Popenoe's piece makes no reference to this bridge or this gorge at all, only a short general passage on the Atlantic-to-Pacific rail system — so the page caption is independent picture-desk copy, not a description grounded in anything Popenoe wrote. The bridge is identifiable independently, by its engineering record, as the same Pacific-line Río Grande crossing documented under construction and in service in Plates I and II. Also sits alongside the other Land of the Banana material already catalogued elsewhere in this archive (the Talamanca ethnographic photographs by H. Wimmer and H. N. Rudd, pp. 211–212) — included here instead because its subject is this bridge, not Talamanca life.

Three Registers, One Structure

A souvenir postcard documenting active construction; a souvenir postcard documenting triumphant completion; one National Geographic plate crediting a different photo agency than the rest of its feature, most likely a simple matter of who held the negative. All three show the same record-setting bridge — for decades the highest railway crossing in Central America — but only the first two were made by anyone with a direct, ongoing connection to the work itself.

Costa Rica Histórica — Puente Río Grande
